title: The Strait That Isn't: Iranian 'Shipping Management' Collapsed Real Traffic by 90%+ While Claiming Control
subtitle: AIS data doesn't lie. Tehran says everything is fine. The satellites say otherwise.
author: The Siphoned Truth
content:
On paper, the Strait of Hormuz is open for business. Iranian officials say so. Al Jazeera reports it. The Pentagon says the waterway remains free for international commerce. And yet — the numbers tell a different story.

Since February 2026, AIS satellite tracking data shows commercial shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has collapsed by 87 to 97 percent. The waterway, which normally handles roughly 175 vessel transits per day — tankers, cargo ships, LNG carriers — is down to between 6 and 22 confirmed daily transits. That's not a slowdown. That's a shutdown.

The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO), which monitors the region independently, confirms the picture. Its latest advisory describes "extremely limited" commercial traffic with just three confirmed transits on record. Three. In a corridor that normally moves 20 percent of the world's oil supply.

The gap between what Tehran says and what the data shows is not a rounding error. It's the widest disconnect in modern maritime surveillance history.

The Disconnect: 'Open' and 'Under Control'

On one side: the public statements. Iran's naval commanders insist they are "managing" shipping through the strait in an orderly fashion. State media frames Iranian naval presence as a stabilizing force — proof that Tehran, not Washington, is the responsible steward of the world's most contested shipping lane.

On the other side: the AIS pings. Automated Identification System transponders, which most commercial vessels broadcast to avoid collisions, are showing near-silence. Even where signals do appear, there's reason for skepticism. Researchers have identified at least 62 vessels exhibiting AIS spoofing — deliberately transmitting false positions to disguise actual movements. Ships are turning off their tracking beacons, falsifying their coordinates, or simply going dark.

This isn't random. It's a pattern. When shipping companies don't trust a corridor enough to broadcast their position, it's because they don't trust the corridor at all.

The Physical Evidence

The gap in AIS coverage may actually understate the collapse. Maritime analysts note that satellite AIS has known gaps — particularly in areas with overlapping coastal jurisdiction and heavy military activity. Vessels in certain zones may simply not be picked up by orbital receivers. "We're missing half the picture," one industry analyst noted on LinkedIn, reviewing the data. "The real number is probably lower."

Independent maritime intelligence groups tracking the region have cross-referenced AIS voids with radar pass data and arrived at similar conclusions: whatever traffic remains is operating under conditions no sane shipping company would willingly accept.

The insurance market agrees. Lloyd's and other maritime insurers have reportedly spiked premiums for Hormuz transit by more than 400 percent. That number alone tells you everything about the real-world risk assessment. Insurers don't speculate. They price actuarially verifiable danger. A 400-percent premium increase means the underwriters see something the official statements are not showing.

The Military Footnote

The United States has not been idle. The Pentagon has deployed additional carrier groups to the region. The State Department continues to insist the strait remains open. US Navy vessels have engaged Iranian tankers directly — documented confrontations occurred on May 8th, 2026, as US forces intercepted vessels suspected of transporting sanctioned cargo.

But there's an operational contradiction worth noting: if the strait is truly "open" and functioning normally, the carrier deployments make little strategic sense. A carrier group in the Gulf is an enormous, expensive, politically charged signal. The United States does not station two carrier strike groups opposite Iranian waters because it's worried about paperwork.

The verbal hedge — "open, but we're watching" — is the language of a government trying to have it both ways. It acknowledges the problem without conceding the scope. It reassures allies without alarming markets. And it sets up the inevitable "we told you so" if things deteriorate further.

What the Data Actually Shows

The numbers are not ambiguous:

  • February 2026 baseline: ~175 daily transits
  • Current confirmed AIS transits: 6–22 per day
  • Collapse range: 87–97%
  • Vessels with suspicious AIS spoofing: 62 and counting
  • UKMTO-verified commercial transits: 3 confirmed
  • Insurance premium increase: 400%+

This is not a temporary disruption. It is a structural collapse in a critical global supply route, happening in real time, while the parties responsible for it publicly insist everything is under control.

The Bottom Line

Tehran is managing a strait that ships have decided to stop using. That is the actual story. The framing of "orderly control" is not inaccurate in a narrow sense — there is order, because there is almost no traffic. The canal is empty because the risk is too high, the insurance too expensive, and the diplomatic cover too thin to justify the passage.

When the waterway that moves a fifth of the world's oil goes dark, and the authority controlling it says "trust us, it's fine" — the AIS data says otherwise. And AIS doesn't do press releases.
sources: AIS satellite tracking data (multiple providers, February–May 2026)
UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) advisories, May 2026
Pentagon and US State Department public statements, May 2026
Maritime insurance market reports (Lloyd's syndicate data, May 2026)
LinkedIn maritime analyst community discussion, May 2026
